---
description: "Backlog stage rules for triage, prioritization, and scope shaping."
applyTo: "docs/00-09-admin/03-planning-workspace/backlog/**/*.md"
---

# planning workspace — backlog

## purpose

Triage inbox items into a prioritized backlog with clear intent, ownership direction, and an initial scope shape.

## entry criteria

Work belongs in backlog when:

- it has been captured and is ready for triage
- initial clarification is complete or scoped enough to proceed
- it requires prioritization and ownership routing

## allowed operations

- assign a tentative owner or owning role
- set priority and target milestone/sprint (if used)
- refine the problem statement and define intended outcome
- identify dependencies and constraints
- split oversized items into smaller backlog entries
- decide whether the item should proceed to planning (plans)

## exit criteria

Move an item out of backlog when:

- the problem/outcome is sufficiently clear to plan
- priority and ownership direction are decided

Typical exits:

- backlog → plans (ready for structured planning)
- backlog → archive (rejected/duplicate/out of scope, with reason)

## required artifacts

A backlog item should include:

- outcome statement (what success looks like)
- scope outline (in/out at a high level)
- priority rationale
- ownership direction
- dependencies/risks (if known)

## handoff expectations

When moving to plans:

- include decision points that must be resolved
- identify impacted paths/domains
- propose an approach options list (at least 1–2 options)

## guardrails

- do not start implementation from backlog
- do not accept “hidden work”; keep scope visible
- avoid jumping into detailed solution design before plans

---
